title: Needlestick Injury & BBV Exposure
oneLiner: An occupational or accidental percutaneous injury with potential HIV, HBV, or HCV transmission – urgent risk assessment and post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) must begin within hours to prevent seroconversion.

sections.Needlestick Injury & Bloodborne Virus Ex: **One-liner:** An occupational or accidental percutaneous injury with potential HIV, HBV, or HCV transmission – urgent risk assessment and post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) must begin within hours to prevent seroconversion.
sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Delayed HIV PEP initiation (>72 hours):** PEP is ineffective after 72h – risk-stratify and consider starting within 1 hour if high-risk exposure; the deciding factor is source risk (e.g. known HIV+, MSM, IVDU, high viral load) and injury severity (hollow-bore needle, deep, visible blood).
- **Acute anaphylaxis to PEP drugs (rare but possible):** watch for urticaria, angioedema, wheeze, hypotension within minutes of first dose – this is a mimic of anxiety but requires immediate IM adrenaline if present.
- **Missed HBV prophylaxis (HBIG + vaccine window <48h):** if source is HBsAg-positive or high-risk, hepatitis B immunoglobulin (HBIG) must be given within 48 hours (ideally within 24h) – failure to give it increases chronic HBV risk significantly.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Sharp prick/cut sensation with visible puncture wound (often small)
  - Anxiety, overwhelming fear of seroconversion, palpitations
  - May be asymptomatic – presenting only because of “dirty” needle exposure
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Small puncture site ± bruising; check for visible blood on needle
  - Assess wound depth, whether it bled spontaneously (higher risk if it did)
  - Tetanus status? Dirty needle may require tetanus vaccine
  - Source patient (if known) – assess risk behaviours, serology status
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Low-risk exposure** (e.g. solid needle, no visible blood, source HIV-negative) → anxiety reaction only; no PEP needed
- **High-risk exposure** (hollow-bore needle, deep injury, source with BBV risk) → requires PEP, urgent source testing, and counselling
- **Tetanus-prone wound** – consider tetanus vaccine ± immunoglobulin if >10 years since last dose or wound heavily contaminated
- **Other bloodborne virus (e.g. HTLV, malaria, syphilis)** – rare, but consider if source from endemic area or high-risk group
- **Splitter:** The single feature that differentiates high- vs low-risk is **source risk status + injury type**: if source is known HIV-negative and needle is solid (e.g. suture needle), risk is negligible; if source is unknown or high-risk, treat as high-risk until proven otherwise.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - **Wound care:** Do not squeeze – wash with soap and water, irrigate with saline or water, cover with waterproof dressing
  - **First aid** – encourage gentle bleeding (do not actively suck), then clean
  - **Take full exposure history:** date/time of injury, source patient details, type of needle, depth, whether blood visible
  - **Consent** – obtain consent for source patient testing (if possible) – this is often the most efficient way
- **Bloods (on exposed person, after counselling and consent):**
  - Baseline HIV, HBV (HBsAg, anti-HBs, anti-HBc), HCV antibody – send urgently
  - Also send stored serum for later testing if source results are pending
  - For HBV: check if vaccinated (anti-HBs >10 IU/L) – if not, need HBIG + vaccine
- **Source patient testing (if available, with consent):**
  - Rapid HIV test, HBsAg, HCV antibody – results within 1-2 hours are invaluable
- **Never-miss test:** **Source patient serology** – if the source is known, testing them for HIV/HBV/HCV is the single most important step to avoid unnecessary PEP or to justify its continuation. Without source results, you may have to give 4 weeks of PEP unnecessarily.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Wound care** (as above) – do not delay for source testing
  - **Risk stratification** (use local algorithm – e.g. “high/medium/low/negligible”)
  - **HIV PEP** – if high-risk exposure: start as soon as possible, ideally within 1 hour. **Do not wait for source results if they are delayed** – you can stop later if source is negative.
  - **HBV prophylaxis** – if source HBsAg-positive or high-risk, give HBIG IM (different site to vaccine) + start HBV vaccine series (if not already immune)
  - **HCV** – no immediate PEP; monitor with HCV RNA at 4-6 weeks
  - **Tetanus** – assess vaccination history; give tetanus vaccine ± tetanus immunoglobulin (TIG) if dirty wound and >10 years since last dose
- **Ward / first 24-72h:**
  - **HIV PEP continuation** – if started, give for 28 days. Monitor drug side effects (nausea, headache, rash). Refer to Infectious Diseases / Genitourinary Medicine (GUM) for follow-up within 24h
  - **Source results** – if source tests negative for HIV/HBV/HCV, stop PEP and reassure
  - **HCV follow-up** – repeat HCV RNA at 4-6 weeks and 12 weeks; do not use PCR earlier as it may be negative early
  - **HBV follow-up** – complete vaccine series (3 doses over 6 months); check anti-HBs at 1-2 months post-vaccination
  - **Counselling** – provide avoidance advice: use condoms, avoid blood/organ donation, do not share needles/toothbrushes, do not breastfeed (if applicable) until seroconversion ruled out (usually 6 months)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **HIV** – repeat HIV serology at 6 weeks, 12 weeks, and 6 months post-exposure (some guidelines recommend 4th generation testing at 6 weeks is sufficient). If PEP started, test 3 months after completing PEP.
  - **HBV** – if vaccinated and anti-HBs >10, no further action. If not immune, complete vaccine series and check anti-HBs.
  - **HCV** – if negative at 12 weeks, discharge; if positive, refer for antiviral therapy (now curable)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** Not typically admitted unless PEP side effects severe or psychological distress – if admitted: usual observations (4-hourly), clear fluids/light diet, give PEP at same time daily, VTE prophylaxis not routinely needed (unless other risk factors). Most patients are managed as outpatients after initial ED assessment.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
**HIV PEP (take [check local policy] for exact regimen – common example below):**
- First-line (if no known resistance):  
  - *Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate (TDF) 245mg + emtricitabine (FTC) 200mg* fixed-dose combination (Truvada) **OR** *tenofovir alafenamide (TAF) 25mg* + emtricitabine 200mg (Descovy) – **one tablet daily**  
  - **Plus** *raltegravir 400mg* **twice daily** (or dolutegravir 50mg once daily)  
  - Duration: 28 days; start within 1 hour of exposure, do not delay  
- Alternatives: darunavir/ritonavir if resistance suspected (consult ID)
- **Key contraindications:** Severe renal impairment (eGFR <30) – use alternative NRTI (e.g. abacavir, but check HLA-B*5701). Pregnancy – consult ID; tenofovir is safe but raltegravir may be preferred.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Starting PEP without baseline serology** – you must have a baseline HIV test (after consent) to document negative status before exposure; if patient is already HIV-positive, you need a different regimen. Also, **stopping PEP early** (<28 days) because of side effects – counsel heavily to complete course.

**HBV Prophylaxis:**
- HBIG (hepatitis B immunoglobulin) – dose: 500 IU IM (adult) as soon as possible, within 48h. *Dose [check local policy]*  
- Hepatitis B vaccine – 20 mcg (1mL) IM (Engerix B or similar) at day 0, 1 month, 6 months  
- **Key contraindication:** None significant; vaccine safe in immunosuppression. HBIG safe in all.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving HBIG into same injection site as vaccine** – they must be given at different sites (e.g. opposite deltoids). Also, **forgetting booster vaccine** if patient is a known non-responder (anti-HBs <10) – need full 3-dose course.

**Tetanus Prophylaxis:**
- If wound dirty or high risk and last dose >10 years: tetanus vaccine (0.5mL IM) + tetanus immunoglobulin (250-500 IU IM, different site)  
- If completely immunised (3 doses) and last dose <5 years: no need  
- *Dose [check local policy]*
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Seroconversion (HIV, HBV, HCV) – most feared, but rare with timely PEP
  - PEP side effects: nausea, vomiting, headache, diarrhoea, rash – common; can lead to non-compliance
  - Anaphylaxis to HBIG (very rare)
  - Psychological distress: anxiety, depression, acute stress reaction
- **Chronic:**
  - Chronic HBV carrier (if not immune and exposed to HBsAg-positive source)
  - Chronic HCV (if not treated, risk of cirrhosis, HCC)
  - Latent HIV (if PEP fails or not given)
sections.Prognosis:
- **HIV transmission risk:** 0.3% per needlestick (hollow-bore, blood-filled) – reduced to near 0% if PEP started within 1h. Late start increases risk.
- **HBV transmission risk:** 6-30% (if source HBsAg-positive and recipient unvaccinated) – virtually eliminated by HBIG + vaccine within 48h.
- **HCV transmission risk:** 1.8% per needlestick – no PEP, but spontaneous clearance occurs in ~25-30%; chronic HCV is now curable with direct-acting antivirals.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check source patient serology if possible** – it often avoids unnecessary PEP (and its side effects) and reassures the exposed person.
- **Give the first dose of HIV PEP in the ED yourself** – do not send the patient to pharmacy and wait; use ED stock if available.
- **Document everything** – injury details, time, risk assessment, discussion with senior/ID, consent for source testing and for baseline HIV test (note: HIV testing is routine, but must be done with consent). This protects you in future claims.
- **Arrange formal occupational health follow-up** within 24h – they will coordinate blood tests, ensure PEP compliance, and manage side effects.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Starting HIV PEP more than 72h after exposure** – it is ineffective and should not be given except in exceptional circumstances (e.g. high-risk exposure with known drug-resistant virus – consult ID). Wasting drugs and causing side effects for no benefit is harmful.
- **Omitting HBIG for HBV exposure because you assume vaccine alone is sufficient** – HBIG provides immediate passive immunity and must be given within 48h for high-risk exposures (e.g. source HBsAg+). Vaccine alone takes weeks to work.
- **Forgetting to test for HCV RNA** – HCV antibody alone can remain negative for 6-8 weeks, and early detection of acute HCV allows treatment which prevents chronic infection. Do not rely on antibody alone in the first weeks.
- **Not reporting the incident** – every trust has a mandatory incident reporting system. Failure to report can lead to disciplinary action and missed follow-up.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Fever, sore throat, lymphadenopathy (HIV seroconversion illness) – especially 2-6 weeks post-exposure
  - Jaundice, dark urine, right upper quadrant pain (acute HBV/HCV)
  - Severe PEP side effects: persistent vomiting, severe diarrhoea, inability to keep medication down
  - Signs of depression or suicidal ideation – anxiety can be overwhelming
- **Follow-up:**
  - Occupational health clinic (usually GUM/ID) – within 24h for PEP check, then at 2 weeks, 6 weeks, 12 weeks, 6 months
  - For HBV: complete vaccine series at 1 and 6 months; check anti-HBs at 7 months
  - For HCV: repeat HCV RNA at 4-6 weeks and 12 weeks; if negative, discharge; if positive, refer to hepatology
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ID consultant if:**
  - Source patient is known to have HIV drug resistance – PEP regimen may need adjustment (e.g. boosted protease inhibitor instead of integrase inhibitor)
  - Exposed person is pregnant or breastfeeding – requires specialist ID/obstetric input for PEP choice
  - Exposed person has severe renal impairment (eGFR <30) – alternative NRTI needed (e.g. abacavir, after HLA-B*5701 testing)
  - Injury is from a laboratory strain of HIV or a high-titre exposure (e.g. large volume, deep, or from culture) – consider triple PEP with raltegravir + darunavir/ritonavir + a fourth agent
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - Anaphylaxis to PEP drug or HBIG – airway, breathing, circulation compromise
- **NEWS2 / lactate / GCS threshold:** Not specific to needlestick, but if patient develops sepsis-like symptoms (high NEWS, fever, leukopenia) within 2-6 weeks, suspect acute HIV seroconversion and escalate to ID.

rawMarkdown:
## Needlestick Injury & Bloodborne Virus Exposure
**One-liner:** An occupational or accidental percutaneous injury with potential HIV, HBV, or HCV transmission – urgent risk assessment and post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) must begin within hours to prevent seroconversion.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Delayed HIV PEP initiation (>72 hours):** PEP is ineffective after 72h – risk-stratify and consider starting within 1 hour if high-risk exposure; the deciding factor is source risk (e.g. known HIV+, MSM, IVDU, high viral load) and injury severity (hollow-bore needle, deep, visible blood).
- **Acute anaphylaxis to PEP drugs (rare but possible):** watch for urticaria, angioedema, wheeze, hypotension within minutes of first dose – this is a mimic of anxiety but requires immediate IM adrenaline if present.
- **Missed HBV prophylaxis (HBIG + vaccine window <48h):** if source is HBsAg-positive or high-risk, hepatitis B immunoglobulin (HBIG) must be given within 48 hours (ideally within 24h) – failure to give it increases chronic HBV risk significantly.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Sharp prick/cut sensation with visible puncture wound (often small)
  - Anxiety, overwhelming fear of seroconversion, palpitations
  - May be asymptomatic – presenting only because of “dirty” needle exposure
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Small puncture site ± bruising; check for visible blood on needle
  - Assess wound depth, whether it bled spontaneously (higher risk if it did)
  - Tetanus status? Dirty needle may require tetanus vaccine
  - Source patient (if known) – assess risk behaviours, serology status

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Low-risk exposure** (e.g. solid needle, no visible blood, source HIV-negative) → anxiety reaction only; no PEP needed
- **High-risk exposure** (hollow-bore needle, deep injury, source with BBV risk) → requires PEP, urgent source testing, and counselling
- **Tetanus-prone wound** – consider tetanus vaccine ± immunoglobulin if >10 years since last dose or wound heavily contaminated
- **Other bloodborne virus (e.g. HTLV, malaria, syphilis)** – rare, but consider if source from endemic area or high-risk group
- **Splitter:** The single feature that differentiates high- vs low-risk is **source risk status + injury type**: if source is known HIV-negative and needle is solid (e.g. suture needle), risk is negligible; if source is unknown or high-risk, treat as high-risk until proven otherwise.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - **Wound care:** Do not squeeze – wash with soap and water, irrigate with saline or water, cover with waterproof dressing
  - **First aid** – encourage gentle bleeding (do not actively suck), then clean
  - **Take full exposure history:** date/time of injury, source patient details, type of needle, depth, whether blood visible
  - **Consent** – obtain consent for source patient testing (if possible) – this is often the most efficient way
- **Bloods (on exposed person, after counselling and consent):**
  - Baseline HIV, HBV (HBsAg, anti-HBs, anti-HBc), HCV antibody – send urgently
  - Also send stored serum for later testing if source results are pending
  - For HBV: check if vaccinated (anti-HBs >10 IU/L) – if not, need HBIG + vaccine
- **Source patient testing (if available, with consent):**
  - Rapid HIV test, HBsAg, HCV antibody – results within 1-2 hours are invaluable
- **Never-miss test:** **Source patient serology** – if the source is known, testing them for HIV/HBV/HCV is the single most important step to avoid unnecessary PEP or to justify its continuation. Without source results, you may have to give 4 weeks of PEP unnecessarily.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Wound care** (as above) – do not delay for source testing
  - **Risk stratification** (use local algorithm – e.g. “high/medium/low/negligible”)
  - **HIV PEP** – if high-risk exposure: start as soon as possible, ideally within 1 hour. **Do not wait for source results if they are delayed** – you can stop later if source is negative.
  - **HBV prophylaxis** – if source HBsAg-positive or high-risk, give HBIG IM (different site to vaccine) + start HBV vaccine series (if not already immune)
  - **HCV** – no immediate PEP; monitor with HCV RNA at 4-6 weeks
  - **Tetanus** – assess vaccination history; give tetanus vaccine ± tetanus immunoglobulin (TIG) if dirty wound and >10 years since last dose
- **Ward / first 24-72h:**
  - **HIV PEP continuation** – if started, give for 28 days. Monitor drug side effects (nausea, headache, rash). Refer to Infectious Diseases / Genitourinary Medicine (GUM) for follow-up within 24h
  - **Source results** – if source tests negative for HIV/HBV/HCV, stop PEP and reassure
  - **HCV follow-up** – repeat HCV RNA at 4-6 weeks and 12 weeks; do not use PCR earlier as it may be negative early
  - **HBV follow-up** – complete vaccine series (3 doses over 6 months); check anti-HBs at 1-2 months post-vaccination
  - **Counselling** – provide avoidance advice: use condoms, avoid blood/organ donation, do not share needles/toothbrushes, do not breastfeed (if applicable) until seroconversion ruled out (usually 6 months)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **HIV** – repeat HIV serology at 6 weeks, 12 weeks, and 6 months post-exposure (some guidelines recommend 4th generation testing at 6 weeks is sufficient). If PEP started, test 3 months after completing PEP.
  - **HBV** – if vaccinated and anti-HBs >10, no further action. If not immune, complete vaccine series and check anti-HBs.
  - **HCV** – if negative at 12 weeks, discharge; if positive, refer for antiviral therapy (now curable)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** Not typically admitted unless PEP side effects severe or psychological distress – if admitted: usual observations (4-hourly), clear fluids/light diet, give PEP at same time daily, VTE prophylaxis not routinely needed (unless other risk factors). Most patients are managed as outpatients after initial ED assessment.

### Drugs & Doses

**HIV PEP (take [check local policy] for exact regimen – common example below):**
- First-line (if no known resistance):  
  - *Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate (TDF) 245mg + emtricitabine (FTC) 200mg* fixed-dose combination (Truvada) **OR** *tenofovir alafenamide (TAF) 25mg* + emtricitabine 200mg (Descovy) – **one tablet daily**  
  - **Plus** *raltegravir 400mg* **twice daily** (or dolutegravir 50mg once daily)  
  - Duration: 28 days; start within 1 hour of exposure, do not delay  
- Alternatives: darunavir/ritonavir if resistance suspected (consult ID)
- **Key contraindications:** Severe renal impairment (eGFR <30) – use alternative NRTI (e.g. abacavir, but check HLA-B*5701). Pregnancy – consult ID; tenofovir is safe but raltegravir may be preferred.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Starting PEP without baseline serology** – you must have a baseline HIV test (after consent) to document negative status before exposure; if patient is already HIV-positive, you need a different regimen. Also, **stopping PEP early** (<28 days) because of side effects – counsel heavily to complete course.

**HBV Prophylaxis:**
- HBIG (hepatitis B immunoglobulin) – dose: 500 IU IM (adult) as soon as possible, within 48h. *Dose [check local policy]*  
- Hepatitis B vaccine – 20 mcg (1mL) IM (Engerix B or similar) at day 0, 1 month, 6 months  
- **Key contraindication:** None significant; vaccine safe in immunosuppression. HBIG safe in all.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving HBIG into same injection site as vaccine** – they must be given at different sites (e.g. opposite deltoids). Also, **forgetting booster vaccine** if patient is a known non-responder (anti-HBs <10) – need full 3-dose course.

**Tetanus Prophylaxis:**
- If wound dirty or high risk and last dose >10 years: tetanus vaccine (0.5mL IM) + tetanus immunoglobulin (250-500 IU IM, different site)  
- If completely immunised (3 doses) and last dose <5 years: no need  
- *Dose [check local policy]*

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Seroconversion (HIV, HBV, HCV) – most feared, but rare with timely PEP
  - PEP side effects: nausea, vomiting, headache, diarrhoea, rash – common; can lead to non-compliance
  - Anaphylaxis to HBIG (very rare)
  - Psychological distress: anxiety, depression, acute stress reaction
- **Chronic:**
  - Chronic HBV carrier (if not immune and exposed to HBsAg-positive source)
  - Chronic HCV (if not treated, risk of cirrhosis, HCC)
  - Latent HIV (if PEP fails or not given)

### Prognosis
- **HIV transmission risk:** 0.3% per needlestick (hollow-bore, blood-filled) – reduced to near 0% if PEP started within 1h. Late start increases risk.
- **HBV transmission risk:** 6-30% (if source HBsAg-positive and recipient unvaccinated) – virtually eliminated by HBIG + vaccine within 48h.
- **HCV transmission risk:** 1.8% per needlestick – no PEP, but spontaneous clearance occurs in ~25-30%; chronic HCV is now curable with direct-acting antivirals.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check source patient serology if possible** – it often avoids unnecessary PEP (and its side effects) and reassures the exposed person.
- **Give the first dose of HIV PEP in the ED yourself** – do not send the patient to pharmacy and wait; use ED stock if available.
- **Document everything** – injury details, time, risk assessment, discussion with senior/ID, consent for source testing and for baseline HIV test (note: HIV testing is routine, but must be done with consent). This protects you in future claims.
- **Arrange formal occupational health follow-up** within 24h – they will coordinate blood tests, ensure PEP compliance, and manage side effects.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Starting HIV PEP more than 72h after exposure** – it is ineffective and should not be given except in exceptional circumstances (e.g. high-risk exposure with known drug-resistant virus – consult ID). Wasting drugs and causing side effects for no benefit is harmful.
- **Omitting HBIG for HBV exposure because you assume vaccine alone is sufficient** – HBIG provides immediate passive immunity and must be given within 48h for high-risk exposures (e.g. source HBsAg+). Vaccine alone takes weeks to work.
- **Forgetting to test for HCV RNA** – HCV antibody alone can remain negative for 6-8 weeks, and early detection of acute HCV allows treatment which prevents chronic infection. Do not rely on antibody alone in the first weeks.
- **Not reporting the incident** – every trust has a mandatory incident reporting system. Failure to report can lead to disciplinary action and missed follow-up.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Fever, sore throat, lymphadenopathy (HIV seroconversion illness) – especially 2-6 weeks post-exposure
  - Jaundice, dark urine, right upper quadrant pain (acute HBV/HCV)
  - Severe PEP side effects: persistent vomiting, severe diarrhoea, inability to keep medication down
  - Signs of depression or suicidal ideation – anxiety can be overwhelming
- **Follow-up:**
  - Occupational health clinic (usually GUM/ID) – within 24h for PEP check, then at 2 weeks, 6 weeks, 12 weeks, 6 months
  - For HBV: complete vaccine series at 1 and 6 months; check anti-HBs at 7 months
  - For HCV: repeat HCV RNA at 4-6 weeks and 12 weeks; if negative, discharge; if positive, refer to hepatology

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ID consultant if:**
  - Source patient is known to have HIV drug resistance – PEP regimen may need adjustment (e.g. boosted protease inhibitor instead of integrase inhibitor)
  - Exposed person is pregnant or breastfeeding – requires specialist ID/obstetric input for PEP choice
  - Exposed person has severe renal impairment (eGFR <30) – alternative NRTI needed (e.g. abacavir, after HLA-B*5701 testing)
  - Injury is from a laboratory strain of HIV or a high-titre exposure (e.g. large volume, deep, or from culture) – consider triple PEP with raltegravir + darunavir/ritonavir + a fourth agent
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - Anaphylaxis to PEP drug or HBIG – airway, breathing, circulation compromise
- **NEWS2 / lactate / GCS threshold:** Not specific to needlestick, but if patient develops sepsis-like symptoms (high NEWS, fever, leukopenia) within 2-6 weeks, suspect acute HIV seroconversion and escalate to ID.
